coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based-on UiO-66-NH2 capped carbon dots for nitrite determination
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: He, Ming-Qin
Li, Hong-Wei
Wu, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite is a commonly used food preservative and a water contaminant that has garnered significant attention due to its harmful effects on human health. Developing a simple and sensitive method for determining nitrite levels is crucial for safeguarding public health. In this paper, we present a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor (CDs@UiO-66-NH2), created by combining orange-red-emitting CDs with blue-emitting UiO-66-NH2. This ratiometric probe detects nitrite ions (NO2 −) based on the diazotization reaction between the amino group in UiO-66-NH2 and the target NO2 −, where the blue emission of UiO-66-NH2 is quenched but the orange-red emission of CDs remains stable. The probe demonstrated a detection range of 0.5–20 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.157 μM for NO2 −. Due to the probe’s distinct color changes in response to NO2 −, RGB values can be easily read using a smartphone, enabling ultrasensitive visual detection of NO2 − with an LOD of 0.76 μM. This sensor was successfully applied to detect NO2 − in environmental water samples. Finally, a smartphone-based RGB reading method using CDs@UiO-66-NH2 for visual quantitative detection of NO2 − was proposed, broadening the application of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 in environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite determination
CDs
UiO-66-NH2
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of nitrite ion in actual sample
Preparation and characterization of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2
Responsive mechanism of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 to NO2 −
Applications of CDs@UiO-66 for NO2 − determination in water saurces

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence and (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of RhB and CDs. (c) TEM image of CDs with particle size distribution in the insert, and (d) HRTEM image of CDs. (e) XRD patterns of RhB and CDs, and (f) FT-IR spectra of PEG 400, RhB and CDs; (g) XPS survey scan spectrum, (h) high-resolution XPS spectrum of (g) C1s, and (i) O1s of CDs, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence and (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2, respectively. (c) Comparison of XRD spectra of CDs, UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2; (d) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2, and (e) photographs of the powders of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 under white light and UV light, respectively. (f) FT-IR spectra of CDs, UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2. XPS spectra of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 are in (g) survey, (h) C 1s, (i) N 1s, and (j) Zr 3d, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra, (b) curves fitting on I 454/I 581 values, (c) emission photograph, and (d) CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (0.02 mg mL−1) in response to different concentration of NO2 − at the optimal conditions (λex = 365 nm).

(a) R/B values versus NO2 − concentration (0–60 μM), inset shows corresponding fluorescence images under the irradiation of UV lamp; (b) linear fitting of R/B values versus NO2 − concentration (0–20 μM).

(a–c) Selectivity assays of the detection system for other potential interferents: (a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (0.02 mg mL−1) responding to NO2 − and interferents (both 50 μM), respectively, at optimal conditions; corresponding (b) I 454/I 581 against NO2 − and potential interferents, and (c) photograph of the detection system against NO2 − and interferents under the irradiation of UV light. (d–f) Anti-interference assays of the detection system from other potential interferents: (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (0.02 mg mL−1) responding to NO2 − (50 μM) and interferents (250 μM), respectively, at optimal conditions; corresponding (e) I 454/I 581 against NO2 − and interferents, (f) photograph of the assay system under the irridiation of UV light.

(a) Detailed illustration of the reaction process of NO2 − with the amino acids in the presence of CDs@UiO-66-NH2, (b) FT-IR spectra of several different materials for comparison, (c) two localized magnifications of (b), and (d) time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 at 454 nm before and after reacting with NO2 −.
Schematic illustration of (a) the preparation of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 and (b) its application in fluorescence determination and visual inspection of NO2 −.
The performance of the developed sensing platform for detection of NO2 − in different water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor based-on UiO-66-NH2 capped carbon dots for nitrite determination

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, No. 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite is a commonly used food preservative and a water contaminant that has garnered significant attention due to its harmful effects on human health. Developing a simple and sensitive method for determining nitrite levels is crucial for safeguarding public health. In this paper, we present a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor (CDs@UiO-66-NH2), created by combining orange-red-emitting CDs with blue-emitting UiO-66-NH2. This ratiometric probe detects nitrite ions (NO2 −) based on the diazotization reaction between the amino group in UiO-66-NH2 and the target NO2 −, where the blue emission of UiO-66-NH2 is quenched but the orange-red emission of CDs remains stable. The probe demonstrated a detection range of 0.5–20 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.157 μM for NO2 −. Due to the probe’s distinct color changes in response to NO2 −, RGB values can be easily read using a smartphone, enabling ultrasensitive visual detection of NO2 − with an LOD of 0.76 μM. This sensor was successfully applied to detect NO2 − in environmental water samples. Finally, a smartphone-based RGB reading method using CDs@UiO-66-NH2 for visual quantitative detection of NO2 − was proposed, broadening the application of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 in environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite has a wide range of applications in various fields. It is commonly used as a preservative and flavoring agent in food processing, as a nitrogen fertilizer in agriculture, and as a raw material in industrial processes [1–3]. However, excessive nitrite levels pose significant public health risks, including methemoglobinemia, accidental poisoning, and even intestinal and gastric cancer [4,5]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has classified nitrite as a carcinogen, leading to strict regulations on its use worldwide. In China, the national standard limits the concentration of nitrite ions (NO2 −) in drinking water to 0.1 mg L−1 (2.17 μM). Given the extensive use and high toxicity of NO2 −, developing a visual detection method that enables straightforward NO2 − determination and direct signal reading is essential for environmental safety and public health.

However, current methods for NO2 − detection primarily rely on traditional techniques such as chromatography [6,7], electrochemistry [8,9], UV spectrophotometry [10,11], and fluorescence spectrophotometry [12,13]. In the past decade, fluorescent sensors have garnered significant attention due to their high sensitivity, ease of use, and rapid response times. Despite these advantages, single-signal fluorescence spectroscopy is prone to external interference, which can lead to inaccurate results [14]. In contrast, ratiometric techniques offer a self-calibrating reference signal, significantly reducing interference and enhancing the accuracy of detection [15,16]. Additionally, ratiometric methods often result in clearly distinguishable color changes, making visual inspection and color identification more straightforward [3,17,18]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are currently popular as ratiometric sensors; however, their fluorescence properties typically stem from lanthanide metal ions and complex organic linkers, limiting their applications [17]. To address this limitation, the host–guest assembly method has been rapidly developed. This approach involves preparing MOF composites by encapsulating fluorescent guests within the cavities of MOFs [19,20]. UiO-66, in particular, are excellent substrates for this purpose due to their tunable pore sizes and exceptionally high specific surface areas, making them ideal for encapsulating a variety of guest substances such as fluorescent dyes, quantum dots, and carbon dots [21].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a promising class of novel carbon nanomaterials that are widely used in fields such as catalysis, sensing, and bioimaging due to their exceptional photoluminescent properties [22–24]. Compared to traditional phosphors like fluorescent dyes and nanocrystals, CDs offer several advantages, including biocompatibility, ease of synthesis and functionalization, excellent aqueous solubility, and chemical stability, particularly resistance to photobleaching [25,26]. Given these attributes, constructing ratiometric probes for NO2 − monitoring using MOFs that encapsulate CDs with significantly different emissions as reference signal probes is highly feasible.

In this paper, we present a novel ratiometric fluorescent sensor, CDs@UiO-66-NH2, designed for the detection of NO2 − (Scheme 1 a). UiO-66-NH2, an amino-functionalized version of UiO-66 with high chemical stability, emits blue fluorescence at 454 nm and serves as the response probe. The CDs, derived from Rhodamine B (RhB) and PEG 400, emits stable orange-red fluorescence at 579 nm, providing a reference signal through interaction with UiO-66-NH2. Under strongly acidic conditions, the amino group of UiO-66-NH2 reacts with NO2 − via a diazotization reaction, which is highly specific and sensitive. This reaction produces diazonium salts that quench the blue emission of UiO-66-NH2 while maintaining the orange-red emission of the CDs. As a result, a clear color transition is observed, changing from blue to purple, and finally to orange-red. Notably, by using a smartphone-based analyzer, ultrasensitive quantitative visual analysis of NO2 − can be achieved by measuring the R/B (red/blue) values (Scheme 1b).

Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) with a purity of 99 % or greater was obtained from Macklin Reagent in Shanghai, China. 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC), isophthalic acid (IPA), sodium nitrite (NaNO2) with a purity of at least 98 %, and chromatography-grade ethanol, all with purities of 99 % or higher, were sourced from Aladdin in Shanghai, China. Analytical grade methanol (AR) was sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent in Shanghai, China. Analytical grade hydrochloric acid (AR) was acquired from Xilong Chemical Reagent in Guangzhou, China. Analytical grade N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was obtained from Tianjin Tiantai Chemical Company Limited in Tianjin, China. Besides, Rhodamine B (RhB) was purchased from TCI (Shang hai, China) (purity ≥98 %), and PEG 400 (AR) was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory Limited (Tianjin, China).

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan), and infrared spectra were collected with a VERTEX 80V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on a Rigaku D/max2550 diffractometer, utilizing Cu-Kα radiation and scanning at a rate of 3° per minute over a 2θ range of 4 –70°. UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Additionally, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out with a Hitachi Regulus 8100, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a JEM-2200FS (Jeol Ltd., Japan). Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were measured using an iPore 400 specific surface area and pore size analyzer. Before the measurements, the samples were degassed under a dynamic vacuum at 200 °C for 4 h. Specific surface area values were determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a THERMO SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi with a monochromatic Al target. Additionally, Zeta-potential measurements were carried out using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK).

Rhodamine B (RhB) derived CDs were prepared according to a reported synthetic methods [27]. Specifically, PEG 400 (0.67 mL) and RhB (20.0 mg) were dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (10.0 mL). This mixture was then heated in a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon lining at 180 °C for 8 h (Scheme 1a). After naturally cooling to room temperature (RT), the ethanol was evaporated under reduced pressure at 55 °C, resulting in an orange viscous solid. This solid was re-dissolved and purified by dialysis in ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 100) for 6 h, after larger particles being removed by filtration through a 0.45 μm membrane. The final product was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for future use.

The synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 followed a previously reported method with slight modifications. [28,29]. To achieve this, 0.57 mmol solid ZrCl4 was dissolved in 7.2 mL of DMF, followed by the addition of 1.8 mL concentrated hydrochloric acid. The resulting solution was ultrasonicated in a water bath at room temperature until complete dissolution of ZrCl4 was achieved. Subsequently, 0.285 mmol of NH2-BDC and IPA were introduced into the solution, and the total volume was brought up to 18 mL using DMF. Following sonification for 30 min, the mixture was transferred into a pressure–resistant bottle and heated at 120 °C for 24 h. Upon returning to RT, the precipitate was filtered and washed 3 times with 5 mL DMF, followed by 5 mL methanol for 3 times. Finally, the precipitate was dried at 60 °C under vacuum.

100 mg of UiO-66-NH2 was added to a reaction flask containing 8 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated until fully dissolved. Then, 2 mL of CDs solution (0.5 mL⋅mL−1) was added, and the mixture was heated and stirred at 60 °C for 12 h. After cooling to RT, the precipitate was filtered and washed several times with ultrapure water until there was no fluorescent signal in the cleaning fluid. Finally, the precipitate was centrifuged and dried under vacuum at 60 °C to obtain CDs@UiO-66-NH2.

4 mg of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 powder was dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water and ultrasonicated for 10 min to obtain a suspension of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 nanoparticles, which was then used as a sensing probe for NO2 − detection. The experiments were conducted by mixing CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (0.02 mg mL−1) and sulfuric acid solution (1.5 mM) in ultrapure water with varying concentrations of NO2 −, to reach a final volume of 1 mL. The corresponding fluorescence spectrum was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, after incubation at 70 °C for 60 min

Under the irradiation of UV light, the RGB values of the samples were read directly using the Colorimeter App of smartphone, which were then processed to obtain the R/B values (as shown in Fig. S1). Based on the standard curve between R/B values and NO2 − concentrations established, the NO2 − content in sample was obtained based on the R/B values read by the smartphone App.

The selectivity and anti-interference studies were conducted as follows: various interfering substances, including NaCl, KBr, KI, MgSO4, Na2CO3, NaNO3, CaCl2, CuSO4, AlCl3, Ni(NO3)2, arginine, methionine, threonine, isoleucine, serine, glycine, leucine, and others, were prepared as 10 mM solutions and detected using CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (0.02 mg mL−1) as the sensing probe. In the selectivity experiments, the final concentration of each interfering substance was 50 μM; while in the anti-interference experiments, it was 250 μM. A final concentration of 50 μM NO2 − was used as the control group for both experiments. All other steps were performed the same as described in Sections 2.4 and 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitrite ion in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 as a NO2 − sensing probe was evaluated by analyzing samples from lake, river, tap, and drinking water. Lake water was collected from Lake Qing, Lake Yan in the Qianwei District of Jilin University, and South Lake in Changchun. River water was obtained from the Yitong River and Bayi Reservoir in Changchun. Tap water was sourced from the Changchun Water Supply Company, and drinking water was obtained from a Qingdao Jizhimei water purifier. The geographical locations of these sources are shown in Fig. S2. The water samples were prepared by centrifugation and filtered with a 0.45 μm membrane to remove impurities before quantitative analysis. The recoveries were assessed by spiking the samples with standard solutions at different concentrations (0, 10, 15 μM). All analyses were conducted according to the procedures outlined in Sections 2.4 and 2.5.

CDs were synthesized using PEG 400 and rhodamine B as carbon sources in anhydrous ethanol, following a reported hydrothermal synthesis method [27]. As shown in Fig. 1 a, RhB exhibits an emission peak at 577 nm, while the synthesized CDs have a peak at 579 nm under 365 nm excitation, indicating only a slight difference. Additionally, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of RhB and the CDs are either very similar (Fig. 1b), which aligns well with literature reports and suggests that the emission is primarily molecular [27]. Then, the surface morphology and structure of CDs were characterized by employing TEM, XRD, FT-IR spectrum, and XPS, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 1c, the CDs are spherical and well dispersed with an average particle size of 1.30 ± 0.32 nm. The HRTEM image of the CDs (Fig. 1d) reveals a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the graphitic carbon (100) facet [30] and confirming the formation of carbon core. Notably, the XRD pattern (Fig. 1e) shows a broad band centered at 21.6° (d = 0.411 nm), in contrast to the crystalline structure of the precursor RhB completely. The peak position of it is consistent with the crystallographic spacing of graphite (002) [31], which validate again the existence of carbon core. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1f) reveals several characteristic bands: beyond the prominent alkyl absorption peak from PEG 400 at 2859 cm−1 and the COC absorption peak at 1109 cm−1, as well as the N-aryl stretching mode at 1355 cm−1, the CDs retain some unsaturated bands typical of the carbon core, such as CO (1755 cm−1), CN (1645 cm−1), and CC (1590 cm−1) [27,31,32]. Besides, as can be seen from Fig. S3 and Table S1, the CDs have only one fluorescence lifetime of 2.60 ns. Therefore, it indicates that the emission source of the CDs likely originates from molecular states, which are formed during the pyrolysis of RhB molecules or fragments on the carbon nucleus [27].

Finally, the elemental composition and chemical bonding in CDs were characterized using XPS (Fig. 1g, h and i). The full range XPS spectrum (Fig. 1g) illustrates that the CDs are mainly composed of C and O, with very low and rare amount of N (Fig. 1g). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum, C1s (Fig. 1h) is mainly present in the form of CC (284.8 eV), CN (286.3 eV) and COC/CO (286.8 eV) [33,34]; while O1s (Fig. 1i) is mainly present in the form of CO (532.2 eV), COC (532.6 eV) and OH (533.1 eV) [33,35,36]. Taken all together, such results indicate that CDs, processing small particle size with emission and COOH functional group at surface were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: UiO-66-NH2 was prepared using previously reported method with slight modifications [28,29]. The XRD spectra shown in Fig. S4 confirm the successful preparation of UiO-66-NH2, with the key diffraction peaks at (111), (002), (006), and (860) closely matching the reported results [37]. Of note, the CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was prepared by mixing of the as-prepared CDs into the suspension of UiO-66-NH2, following a 12 h stirring at 25 °C to allow them to fully bind. After repeated washing, under the excitation of 365 nm, the emission peak of CDs shifted from 577 nm to 581 nm while that of UiO-66-NH2 shifted from 454 nm to 445 nm (Fig. 2 a). Such shifts indicate the local environmental changes of both emission cores and suggest the binding of CDs with UiO-66-NH2. The optical properties of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 were then evaluated by UV–vis absorption spectrum, and compared with CDs and UiO-66-NH2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2b, the CDs had absorption peaks mainly at 554 nm, whereas UiO-66-NH2 shows peaks at 390 nm and 280 nm. Upon their binding, a new absorption peak at 580 nm appears, suggesting a red-shift from the CDs' original absorption peak at 554 nm. This molecular-like optical transition is due to the quasi-continuous electronic energy structure and quantum confinement effects in both CDs and UiO-66-NH2 [38]. In addition, as shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1, after the combination of CDs and UiO-66-NH2, the fluorescence lifetime splits into two components, specifically are 0.42 ns (accounting for 27.49 %) and 1.78 ns (accounting for 72.51 %). This change in emission lifetime should be attributed to the interaction between the luminescent groups on the surface of the CDs and UiO-66-NH2.

The morphology of the material was then characterized using SEM and TEM. Fig S5a shows that the synthesized UiO-66-NH2 forms heterogeneous aggregates with an atypical cuboctahedral structure. This morphology remains largely unchanged after binding with CDs (Fig. S5b). Additionally, a comparison of the TEM images of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S5c and 5d) reveals that while UiO-66-NH2 retains its intact octahedral shape, the surface becomes rougher after loading with CDs.

To further elucidate the chemical structure of CDs@UiO-66-NH2, XRD, FT-IR, EDS and XPS were conducted on it, respectively. Notably, the XRD patterns (Fig. 2c) show similar diffraction peaks both for UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2, indicating that the loading of CDs did not alter the crystal structure of UiO-66-NH2. Then the porosities of UiO-66-NH2 and CD@UiO-66-NH2 were evaluated and compared using the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig. 2d). The results show a significant decrease in N2 adsorption for CDs@UiO-66-NH2 compared to that of UiO-66-NH2, after loading with CDs. Specifically, the BET surface areas of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 were evaluated to be 498 m2 g−1 and 289 m2 g−1, respectively. The difference between them suggests that CDs are located on the surfaces, both within the pores and on the outer surfaces of UiO-66-NH2. Besides, Fig. 2e presents photographs of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 powders, both under white and UV light, respectively. The photographs reveal noticeable color differences between the two samples. In particular, the color variations under the same conditions further highlight the compositional differences between them, indirectly confirming the successful construction of the CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite.

The compositions and surface functional groups were then characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2f) for CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and CDs@UiO-66-NH2, respectively. Due to the extremely low concentration of CDs in the composites, unfortunately, their characteristic peaks were not detectable in the FT-IR spectrum of the composite. Notably, the peak at 661 cm−1 corresponds to ZrO bonds [39], and the peak at 1390 cm−1 is associated with the symmetric stretching model of the carboxylic acid [40]. The presence of these peaks in both UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 indicates that the major functional groups of UiO-66-NH2 remain largely unchanged after loading CDs. Additionally, the peak at 1575 cm−1, attributed to the bending mode of NH bonds [41,42], overlaps with the adjacent peak at 1604 cm−1, which corresponds to the breathing mode of aryl groups [43], forming a single peak after the binding of CDs. Meanwhile, the peak at 1660 cm−1, assigned to the stretching mode of CO groups [42], becomes weaker and experiences a blue-shift after the introduction of CDs. This suggests that binding between CDs and UiO-66-NH2 may involve interactions between the NH and CO groups. Furthermore, we evaluated the surface charges of CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 using Zeta-potential measurements, which were found to be 6.8 mV, 37.6 mV, and 39.4 mV, respectively (Fig. S6). The increase in surface potential upon binding indicates an electrostatic interaction between the CDs and UiO-66-NH2.

The composition of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was then analyzed using EDS and XPS. Both EDS spectra (Fig. S7) and the full range XPS spectra (Fig. 2g) confirmed the presence of C, N, O and Zr in UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2. The C1s spectra for UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 are shown in Fig. 2h. The C1s spectrum consists of peaks at CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.2 eV) and OCO (288.6 eV) [44]. The proportion of the CO component decreases after the addition of CDs, being consistent with the FT-IR results (Fig. 2f) and indicating that the interaction site between the CDs and UiO-66-NH2 is essentially via the CO group. Similarly, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2i) shows peaks at NC (399.6 eV) and NH (401.7 eV) [45]. The addition of CDs reduces the proportion of NH, with the precondition that in the XPS N1s signal from CDs being almost negligible. This reduction aligns well with the FT-IR results (Fig. 2f) and suggests that the interaction between CDs and UiO-66-NH2 is largely occur at the NH group. Additionally, the peaks for both NC and NH shift to lower binding energies, possibly being attributed to changes in their local environment caused by the interaction between CDs and UiO-66-NH2. Besides, the Zr3d spectra (Fig. 2j) of UiO-66-NH2 and CDs@UiO-66-NH2 show two peaks at 182.8 eV and 185.2 eV, attributed to Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d3/2, respectively [29]. The ratio of Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d3/2 remains nearly unchanged after the introduction of CDs, excluding that the binding site involves the deficient Zr.

Inspired by the principle that aromatic amines can be diazotized by NO2 − under acidic condition [17], the composite of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was utilized as a fluorescent probe to determine NO2 − in solution. Since the detection system should be operated under acidic condition, sulfuric acid was employed to lower the pH [17]. The results demonstrate that CDs@UiO-66-NH2 exhibits dual emission at 454 nm and 581 nm when being excited at 365 nm (Fig. S8a), with a significant increase observed upon the addition of acid (Fig. S8b). Consequently, the acidic conditions must to be optimized before employing CDs@UiO-66-NH2 for fluorescence-based NO2 − detection.

The results (Fig. S9a, b) indicated that the optimal concentration of sulfuric acid was 1.5 mM. We performed the response CDs@UiO-66-NH2 to NO2 − at different temperatures. The results indicated that, at 25 °C, the reaction was very slow which cannot be finished in 120 min; while at 50 °C, the reaction was accelerated but still cannot reach equilibrium in 120 min; however, when the temperature was raised to 70 °C, the reaction reached equilibrium in 60 min; while at 80 °C, the time required to reach equilibrium was not shortened obviously. Therefore, we chose 70 °C for 60 min as the incubation condition for NO2 − determination (Fig. S10). Then, CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was incubated with different amounts of NO2 − (0–100 μM), in the presence of 1.5 mM sulfuric acid. After incubation at 70 °C for 60 min, the fluorescence spectrum for each sample was recorded (Fig. 3 a). As shown, the fluorescence intensity at 454 nm was decreased, while that at 581 nm for CDs remained constant along with concentration increasing of NO2 −. The intensity ratio of 454 nm to 581 nm (I 454/I 581) displayed a strong linear relationship with the NO2 − concentrations in the range of 0.5–20 μM (R2 = 0.9968; Fig. 3b). The limit of detection (LOD), defined as LOD = 3σ/K (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank and K is the slope of the calibration curve), was calculated to be 0.157 μM for NO2 − based on the proposed sensing platform. Furthermore, the distinct color changes from blue to orange-red were observed (Fig. 3c). Besides, the NO2 − induced color change was demonstrated using the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) colorimetric coordinates (Fig. 3d). The continuous transition from blue to orange-red in the CIE chromaticity diagram with increasing NO2 − concentration provides a visual basis for NO2 − determination using portable equipment.

For comparison, we also used UiO-66-NH2 with single emission at 454 nm to determine NO2 − (Fig. S11). The fluorescence response of it to NO2 − (Fig. S11a) leads to a narrow linear response range (0.5–10.0 μM; Fig. S11b). Besides, there is hardly an obvious emission color change for UiO-66-NH2 response to NO2 − (Fig. S11c, d). Therefore, in comparison to UiO-66-NH2, CDs@UiO-66-NH2 showed significant superiority both in the fluorescence and visual determination of NO2 −.

To expand the application of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 for NO2 − detection in practical scenarios, we conducted visual determination using a color recognition application (Colorimeter APP) installed on a smartphone (Fig. S1). As illustrated in Fig. 4 , the values of red to blue (R/B), obtained by analyzing the color of the image with the Colorimeter APP, increased with the NO2 − concentration. The R/B values exhibited a strong phenomenological correlation with NO2 − concentrations in the range of 2.0–20 μM (R2 = 0.991), and the LOD of 0.76 μM was achieved accordingly. This suggests that NO2 − concentration effectively determined visually using a smartphone. Moreover, the performance of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 for ratiometric fluorescence and visual NO2 − determination was compared with existing methods (Table S2) [1,12,13,17,18,46–52], demonstrating that the developed method not only offers a low LOD but also features a significant color change from blue to orange-red, emphasing highlighting its considerable advantage for visual NO2 − detection.

Subsequently, the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent CDs@UiO-66-NH2 probe for NO2 − detection was assessed. For this evaluation, a range of substances including arginine, methionine, threonine, isoleucine, serine, glycine, leucine, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, CO3 2−, Br−, SO4 2−, and NO3 − were added to the solution containing CDs@UiO-66-NH2, and the change response to each was measured (Fig. 5 a). As shown, only NO2 − induced significant changes in the fluorescence spectra of CDs@UiO-66-NH2, resulting in a noticeable decrease in the I 454/I 581 ratio. In contrast, the addition of other substances had minimal effect on the I 454/I 581 ratios (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, the fluorescence images under UV light (Fig. 5c) revealed that only the solution containing NO2 − changed to an orange-red color, while all other solutions remained blue. These results confirm the high specificity and effectiveness of the CDs@UiO-66-NH2 probe for NO2 − detection.

The presence of other substances alongside NO2 − may impact the accuracy of the assay results. Various ions or molecules could potentially alter the fluorescence properties of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 or its interaction with NO2 −. To assess this, anti-interference experiments were conducted using the CDs@UiO-66-NH2 as probe in the presence of arginine, methionine, threonine, isoleucine, serine, glycine, leucine, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, CO3 2−, Br−, SO4 2−, and NO3 −. Of note, the concentrations of NO2 − and each interfering substance was 50 μM and 250 μM, respectively (Fig. 5d, e). As shown, the fluorescence spectra and I 454/I 581 ratios for the mixtures were reduced, with a similar decreasing trend to that observed with solution containing only NO2 −. Additionally, Fig. 5f demonstrates that, except for the control group, all solutions turned to orange-red under UV light. These results indicate that the ratiometric fluorescence probe of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 exhibits strong anti-interference capability for these potential interferents in NO2 − determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responsive mechanism of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 to NO2 −
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 to sense NO2 − is ether proposed to the diazotization reaction of NH2 with NO2 − [17], with the detailed processes illustrated in Fig. 6 a. To confirm this, FT-IR spectra of each reactant and product were recorded (Fig. 6b). As shown, the addition of NO2 − at both 25 °C and 70 °C resulted in a new absorption peak at 2280 cm−1, being attributed to the accumulation of stretching models of triple azide [3]. Additionally, two bands at 1606 cm−1 and 1557 cm−1 are referred to the stretching model of CC and NH in CDs@UiO-66-NH2, respectively [41–43]. The introduction of NO2 − to the system caused a change in the intensity ratios of these bands, which is mainly attributed to the conversion of NH2 to the azide group. In addition, Fig. 6c compares the fluorescence decay curves for CDs@UiO-66-NH2 before and after reacting with NO2 −. The emission lifetime of the composite was evaluated to be 14.56 ns initially; however, after NO2 − addition, it split into two lifetimes at 0.51 ns (1.61 %) and 14.68 ns (98.39 %), respectively, indicating the occurrence of static quenching (Fig. 6d and Table S3). The XRD spectra of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 before and after NO2 − reaction (Fig. S12a) revealed that UiO-66 maintained its crystal structure, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching was not due to the collapse of the MOF. Furthermore, the fluorescence stability of CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was either investigated upon time (Fig. S12b), showing consistent intensity over one month of storage at room temperature, confirming its adequate stability for fluorescent sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CDs@UiO-66 for NO2 − determination in water saurces
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the developped method was assessed by analyzing NO2 − in various water sources, with recovery rates tested to validate accuracy and reproducibility (Table 1 ). The recovery rates for spiked samples were calculated using the formula of (Measured value-Added value)/Added value × 100 %. Both ratiometric fluorescence and visual methods showed good recovery rates and satisfactory relative standard deviation (RSD) for all the test samples. Specifically, the recovery rates ranged from 95 to 103 % for the ratiometric fluorescence method and 94–108 % for the visual determination method. Notably, the RSDs for ratiometric fluorescence detection and visual inspection were below 3.79 % and 1.95 %, respectively. These results clearly demonstrate that both sensing platforms based on CDs@UiO-66-NH2 probes are highly practical for NO2 − detection in complex real-world samples.

In conclusion, a novel nanosensor for the ratiometric fluorescence monitoring of NO2 − contamination has been successfully developed by combining UiO-66-NH2 with CDs. This sensor enables detection via ratiometric fluorescence signals or color recognition. The specific diazotization reaction between the amino groups of UiO-66-NH2 and NO2 − results in the quenching of the blue fluorescence from UiO-66-NH2, while the orange-red fluorescence of the CDs remains constant. Using the blue fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2 as the target and the orange-red fluorescence of the CDs as the internal standard, a distinct color change from blue to orange-red is observed. The signal outputs from both ratiometric fluorescence and R/B values exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity towards NO2 −, with detection limits of 0.157 μM and 0.76 μM, respectively. The CDs@UiO-66-NH2 probe combines the advantages of ratiometric fluorescence and color recognition, enabling dual determination of NO2 − in real samples. The integration of UiO-66-NH2 with CDs to form a ratiometric fluorescent probe not only extends the application of UiO-66-NH2 as a carrier but also enhances the potential for precise visual detection of small molecules.

Ming-Qin He: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Hong-Wei Li: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yuqing Wu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125422.

ce:section.ce:para:
